executiveSummary:
## Executive Summary: Medicaid Program Performance Analysis

National Medicaid enrollment reached 75.3 million beneficiaries as of January 2026, with most major states experiencing modest enrollment declines following the conclusion of pandemic-era continuous coverage protections. Year-over-year enrollment decreased across nine of the ten largest states, ranging from New York's relatively modest 1.8% decline to Michigan's more substantial 7.6% reduction. Notably, North Carolina bucked this trend with a 0.5% enrollment increase. Texas, with 4.03 million enrollees, experienced a 3.1% year-over-year decline, positioning it as the third-largest state program behind California and New York. National program expenditures totaled $215.9 billion in fiscal year 2024, with a per-enrollee average of $2,869.

Significant variation in per-enrollee spending patterns reflects differing state demographics, benefit structures, and cost management approaches. Pennsylvania leads with $4,377 per enrollee, followed by Massachusetts at $4,201, while Florida maintains the lowest per-enrollee costs at $2,031. Texas ranks in the middle tier with $2,988 per enrollee, approximately 4% above the national average despite having managed care penetration of 93.3%—substantially higher than the national average of 83.2%. Quality performance remains concerning, with national scores averaging 65.6 out of 100 and fifteen states scoring below 60. Texas achieved a moderate quality score of 62, ranking 30th nationally, which represents an opportunity for improvement given the state's high managed care penetration typically associated with better quality outcomes. Recent federal regulatory activity indicates potential enrollment moratoria and new HCBS quality measures for 2028, suggesting increased federal oversight that may impact state program operations and performance expectations.
texasSpotlight:
## Texas Medicaid Program Performance Analysis

Texas operates the nation's third-largest Medicaid program with 4.03 million enrollees, representing a significant portion of the state's healthcare safety net. The program's enrollment declined 3.1% year-over-year, performing better than the national trend and in line with peer states like Pennsylvania (-3.1%) but trailing North Carolina's modest growth (+0.5%). At $2,988 per enrollee, Texas maintains spending levels above the national average of $2,869 but remains cost-effective compared to expansion states like New York ($3,431) and Ohio ($3,561). Notably, Texas demonstrates strong managed care penetration at 93.3%, well above the national average of 83.2%, suggesting effective care coordination infrastructure that likely contributes to its relatively controlled per-capita costs despite serving a large, diverse population.

However, quality outcomes present Texas's most significant challenge, with a score of 62/100 ranking 30th nationally and placing it in the moderate performance tier—well below high-performing states like Massachusetts (81) but avoiding the bottom tier occupied by neighboring southern states like Arkansas (54). This quality gap is particularly concerning given Texas's non-expansion status, which limits coverage for low-income adults and may contribute to care fragmentation. The state's position among southern non-expansion peers shows mixed results: while outperforming traditional laggards, it underperforms relative to its size and managed care sophistication. Key areas to monitor include federal regulatory developments around enrollment moratoria that could affect Texas's large program, ongoing procurement activities that may signal program reforms, and whether the state's managed care investments will translate into measurable quality improvements that close the gap with national leaders.

alerts.title: Pennsylvania Per-Enrollee Spending 53% Above National Average
alerts.description: Pennsylvania's per-enrollee spending of $4,377 significantly exceeds the national average of $2,869, indicating potential cost management issues.

alerts.title: Michigan Medicaid Enrollment Drops 7.6% Year-Over-Year
alerts.description: Michigan experienced a significant enrollment decline of 7.6%, well above the 10% threshold for concern.

alerts.title: Five States Score Below 55 on Quality Measures
alerts.description: Arkansas, South Carolina, Alabama, Oklahoma, and Mississippi all scored critically low on quality measures, with Mississippi at only 48/100.

alerts.title: Florida Achieves Lowest Per-Enrollee Costs at $2,031
alerts.description: Florida demonstrates exceptional cost efficiency with per-enrollee spending 29% below the national average while maintaining large enrollment.

alerts.title: CMS Announces Nationwide Provider Enrollment Moratorium
alerts.description: Federal Register indicates CMS has implemented temporary nationwide moratoriums on provider enrollment, potentially impacting access to care.

executiveInsights.title: CMS announces nationwide temporary enrollment moratorium affecting TX
executiveInsights.summary: Federal Register shows CMS has announced nationwide temporary moratoria on enrollment in Medicare, Medicaid, and CHIP programs. Multiple related announcements appeared in May 2026.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: This could freeze Texas Medicaid enrollment growth and significantly impact HHSC's capacity planning and our contract scope. Immediate client alignment needed on operational adjustments.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Flag enrollment moratorium to HHSC leadership immediately

executiveInsights.title: Texas ranks 30th nationally in quality with 62/100 score
executiveInsights.summary: Texas Medicaid quality score of 62/100 ranks 30th out of 50 states, well below the national average of 65.6 and far from high-performer threshold of 75. Only 15 states scored lower than 60.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Quality performance gaps create immediate improvement opportunities and potential regulatory scrutiny. This positions Accenture's quality improvement capabilities as high-value offerings to HHSC.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Review quality improvement proposals with HHSC quality team

executiveInsights.title: Texas enrollment declining -3.1% YoY with 4.03M current enrollees
executiveInsights.summary: Texas Medicaid enrollment dropped 3.1% year-over-year to 4,032,864 enrollees in January 2026. This decline mirrors the national trend but varies significantly from NC's +0.5% growth.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Declining enrollment reduces program scale and may impact contract economics and HHSC budget projections. Understanding drivers is critical for accurate forecasting and resource planning.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Review enrollment forecasting models with HHSC actuarial team

executiveInsights.title: Texas per-enrollee costs $2,988 vs $2,869 national average
executiveInsights.summary: Texas spends $2,988 per Medicaid enrollee compared to the national average of $2,869, totaling $12.05B in expenditures. This is notably higher than efficient states like Florida ($2,031) and North Carolina ($2,616).
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Above-average per-enrollee costs indicate potential efficiency opportunities worth hundreds of millions. This creates clear value proposition for Accenture's cost management and analytics capabilities.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Flag cost benchmarking analysis to HHSC finance leadership

executiveInsights.title: New 2028 HCBS quality measures set published by CMS
executiveInsights.summary: CMS published new Medicaid Home and Community-Based Services Quality Measure Set for 2028 in the Federal Register on April 28, 2026.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: New HCBS quality measures will require HHSC compliance and reporting system updates. Early preparation gives Accenture advantage in proposing implementation support before competitors respond.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Review 2028 HCBS quality requirements with HHSC HCBS program team

riskOpportunity.label: Enrollment decline impact
riskOpportunity.description: Texas Medicaid enrollment dropping 3.1% YoY may reduce system utilization and contract values but create efficiency opportunities.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Develop cost optimization proposals to maintain margins amid lower enrollment volumes.

riskOpportunity.label: Quality score improvement
riskOpportunity.description: Texas ranks 30th nationally with 62/100 quality score, presenting significant room for performance-based contract wins.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Position quality improvement solutions to help Texas reach top-tier performance levels.

riskOpportunity.label: Managed care recompete
riskOpportunity.description: Texas's 93.3% managed care penetration with 3.8M enrollees represents massive contract renewal opportunities.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Prepare comprehensive managed care system proposals leveraging current HHSC relationships.

riskOpportunity.label: HCBS quality mandates
riskOpportunity.description: New 2028 Home and Community-Based Services quality measures create compliance requirements and system upgrade needs.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Develop HCBS quality measurement and reporting solutions ahead of implementation deadlines.

riskOpportunity.label: OIG oversight intensity
riskOpportunity.description: Active HHS OIG Medicaid portfolio oversight creates compliance risks but also demand for audit-ready systems.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Strengthen compliance monitoring capabilities and proactive audit preparation services.

riskOpportunity.label: Provider enrollment moratorium
riskOpportunity.description: Federal temporary provider enrollment moratoria may disrupt network adequacy and create system reconfiguration needs.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Develop contingency plans for provider network management during enrollment restrictions.

riskOpportunity.label: Per-enrollee cost efficiency
riskOpportunity.description: Texas's $2,988 per-enrollee cost above national average of $2,869 indicates potential cost management opportunities.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Propose cost containment initiatives targeting the $119 per-member gap to national average.

riskOpportunity.label: Competitive procurement landscape
riskOpportunity.description: Active Texas procurement portal and ESBD opportunities signal increased competition for state technology contracts.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Monitor procurement pipelines closely and strengthen differentiation in bid responses.
